content: {"perspective": "Economic", "question": "Round 2 established the Burg-Peet FMD-in-T2D study is NOT cost-effective over the first year in the Dutch healthcare system. This raises a methodological concern: given that IF interventions are essentially free at the consumer margin (no drug cost, no clinician time beyond brief counseling), what portion of the non-cost-effectiveness verdict is driven by the FMD programme's branded food-replacement supplements (Prolon, L-Nutra) rather than by the underlying fasting behavior? In other words, would an unbranded physician-prescribed TRE or 5:2 protocol, which costs essentially zero beyond the patient's own food, likely achieve dominant or near-dominant cost-effectiveness in the same model framework even with modest effect sizes?", "search_queries": ["Prolon L-Nutra branded fasting mimicking diet product cost supplement price per cycle", "cost-effectiveness zero-cost lifestyle intervention dominated strategy dominant diabetes prevention", "time-restricted eating 5:2 free intervention cost per patient per year healthcare utilization", "unbranded fasting protocol counseling cost primary care physician time charge reimbursement", "FMD Prolon supplement cost per month type 2 diabetes program commercial price"]}
